title: Phase Plane Analysis
objectives: Visualize solutions as trajectories in the phase plane
Classify equilibrium points based on eigenvalues
Sketch phase portraits for linear systems

content.text: The **phase plane** provides a geometric way to visualize all solutions of a 2D system simultaneously. Instead of plotting $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ separately against $t$, we plot $y$ versus $x$, showing how the state evolves in the $(x, y)$ plane.

content.title: Phase Plane and Trajectory
content.content:
For a system
$$
\frac{dx}{dt} &= f(x, y) \\[4pt]
\frac{dy}{dt} &= g(x, y)
$$
the **phase plane** is the $xy$-plane where we view $(x, y)$ as the state of the system.

A **trajectory** (or **orbit**) is a curve traced by a solution $(x(t), y(t))$ as $t$ varies.

A **phase portrait** is a collection of representative trajectories showing the qualitative behavior of all solutions.

content.title: Equilibrium Point
content.content:
An **equilibrium point** (or **critical point**, **fixed point**) is a point $(x_0, y_0)$ where $f(x_0, y_0) = g(x_0, y_0) = 0$.

At an equilibrium, $dx/dt = dy/dt = 0$, so the system remains stationary. For $\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}$, the only equilibrium is $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ (assuming $A$ is invertible).

content.title: Direction Field
content.content:
At each point $(x, y)$, the vector $(f(x,y), g(x,y))$ indicates the direction of motion. Plotting these vectors creates the **direction field** or **vector field**.

Trajectories are curves that are everywhere tangent to this field.

content.text: For the linear system $\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}$, the eigenvalues of $A$ completely determine the type of equilibrium at the origin.

content.title: Classification by Eigenvalues
content.content:
Let $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ be the eigenvalues of $A$. The equilibrium at $\mathbf{0}$ is classified as:

**Real Distinct Eigenvalues:**

- **Node** (same sign): Both $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 > 0$ (unstable) or both $< 0$ (stable)

- **Saddle point** (opposite signs): $\lambda_1 < 0 < \lambda_2$ (always unstable)

**Complex Eigenvalues $\alpha \pm \beta i$:**

- **Spiral** (focus): $\alpha \neq 0$; stable if $\alpha < 0$, unstable if $\alpha > 0$

- **Center**: $\alpha = 0$ (pure imaginary eigenvalues); neutrally stable

**Repeated Eigenvalues $\lambda = \lambda_1 = \lambda_2$:**

- **Star node** (proper node): Two independent eigenvectors

- **Degenerate node** (improper node): Only one eigenvector
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Stable Node
content.problem:
Consider $\mathbf{x}' = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{x}$.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = -3$, $\lambda_2 = -1$ (both negative)

Eigenvectors: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$

Solution: $\mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{-3t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{-t}\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$

**Behavior:** All trajectories approach the origin as $t \to \infty$.

- Along $\mathbf{v}_1$ (the $x$-axis), decay is faster ($e^{-3t}$)

- Along $\mathbf{v}_2$ (the $y$-axis), decay is slower ($e^{-t}$)

- As $t \to \infty$, the slower decay dominates, so trajectories become tangent to the $y$-axis near the origin
This is a **stable node** (or **sink**).

content.title: Unstable Node
content.problem:
For $\mathbf{x}' = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{x}$, both eigenvalues are positive.

Trajectories move away from the origin. This is an **unstable node** (or **source**).

content.title: Saddle Point
content.problem:
Consider $\mathbf{x}' = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{x}$.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 1 > 0$, $\lambda_2 = -2 < 0$ (opposite signs)

Eigenvectors: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$

Solution: $\mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{-2t}\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$

**Behavior:**

- Along $\mathbf{v}_1$ (the $x$-axis): trajectories move **away** from origin

- Along $\mathbf{v}_2$ (the $y$-axis): trajectories move **toward** origin

- General trajectories approach along the stable direction, then veer away along the unstable direction

The eigenvector directions are called:

- **Stable manifold**: Direction of $\mathbf{v}_2$ (negative eigenvalue) — trajectories approach origin

- **Unstable manifold**: Direction of $\mathbf{v}_1$ (positive eigenvalue) — trajectories leave origin

This is a **saddle point**, always unstable.

content.title: Stable Spiral
content.problem:
Consider $\mathbf{x}' = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -2 \\ 2 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{x}$.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda = -1 \pm 2i$ (complex with negative real part)

**Behavior:** Solutions spiral toward the origin as $t \to \infty$.

- $|\alpha| = 1$ determines the rate of spiral (decay/growth)

- $\beta = 2$ determines the rotation rate (angular velocity)

- Period of rotation: $T = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \pi$

This is a **stable spiral** (or **stable focus**).

content.title: Unstable Spiral
content.problem: For eigenvalues $\lambda = 1 \pm 2i$ (positive real part), trajectories spiral outward. This is an **unstable spiral**.

content.title: Center
content.problem:
Consider $\mathbf{x}' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ 3 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{x}$.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda = \pm 3i$ (pure imaginary)

Solution involves only $\cos 3t$ and $\sin 3t$ with no exponential growth or decay.

**Behavior:** Trajectories are closed curves (ellipses) around the origin.

This is a **center**, which is neutrally stable—trajectories neither approach nor leave the origin but orbit around it perpetually.

content.title: Star Node
content.problem:
Consider $A = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$.

Eigenvalue $\lambda = -2$ (repeated), with two independent eigenvectors.

Every direction is an eigenvector direction! Trajectories are straight lines through the origin.

This is a **star node** (or **proper node**).

content.title: Degenerate Node
content.problem:
Consider $A = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$.

Eigenvalue $\lambda = -2$ (repeated), with only one eigenvector $\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

Solution: $\mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{-2t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{-2t}\begin{pmatrix} t \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$

Trajectories approach the origin but curve toward the eigenvector direction.

This is a **degenerate node** (or **improper node**).

content.title: Types of Stability
content.content:
An equilibrium $\mathbf{x}_0$ is:

- **Stable**: Trajectories starting near $\mathbf{x}_0$ stay near $\mathbf{x}_0$

- **Asymptotically stable**: Stable, and trajectories approach $\mathbf{x}_0$ as $t \to \infty$

- **Unstable**: Some trajectories starting near $\mathbf{x}_0$ eventually leave any neighborhood

content.title: Stability Criterion for Linear Systems
content.content:
For $\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}$:

1. The origin is **asymptotically stable** $\Leftrightarrow$ all eigenvalues have negative real part

2. The origin is **unstable** $\Leftrightarrow$ at least one eigenvalue has positive real part

3. Centers (pure imaginary eigenvalues) are **stable but not asymptotically stable**
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Complete Phase Portrait Analysis
content.problem:
Sketch the phase portrait for $\mathbf{x}' = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{x}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Eigenvalues.**
\[
\det(A - \lambda I) = (1-\lambda)(-1-\lambda) - 3 = \lambda^2 - 4 = 0
\]
$\lambda_1 = 2$, $\lambda_2 = -2$ (opposite signs — **saddle point**)

**Step 2: Eigenvectors.**

For $\lambda_1 = 2$: $(A - 2I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 3 \\ 1 & -3 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$

$-v_1 + 3v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ (unstable direction)

For $\lambda_2 = -2$: $(A + 2I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$

$v_1 + v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ (stable direction)

**Step 3-6: Sketch.**

- Draw line through origin in direction $(3, 1)$ — **unstable manifold** (arrows away from origin)

- Draw line through origin in direction $(1, -1)$ — **stable manifold** (arrows toward origin)

- Trajectories approach along stable manifold, curve away along unstable manifold

content.text: An alternative to computing eigenvalues explicitly uses the trace and determinant.

content.title: Trace-Determinant Criterion
content.content:
For $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$, let $\tau = \text{tr}(A) = a + d$ and $\Delta = \det(A) = ad - bc$.

The eigenvalues satisfy $\lambda^2 - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$, so:
\[
\lambda = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}
\]

Classification:

- $\Delta < 0$: **Saddle** (eigenvalues real, opposite signs)

- $\Delta > 0$, $\tau^2 - 4\Delta > 0$: **Node** (eigenvalues real, same sign)
 itemize

- $\tau < 0$: Stable node

- $\tau > 0$: Unstable node
 $\Delta > 0$, $\tau^2 - 4\Delta < 0$: **Spiral or center** (complex eigenvalues)
 

- $\tau < 0$: Stable spiral

- $\tau > 0$: Unstable spiral

- $\tau = 0$: Center
itemize
content.label: Theorem

content.text: For nonlinear systems, we can understand local behavior near equilibria by linearization.

content.title: Linearization
content.content:
Consider the nonlinear system $\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ with equilibrium at $\mathbf{x}_0$ (where $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0) = \mathbf{0}$).

The **Jacobian matrix** at $\mathbf{x}_0$ is:
\[
J = \begin{pmatrix} \partial f_1/\partial x & \partial f_1/\partial y \\ \partial f_2/\partial x & \partial f_2/\partial y \end{pmatrix}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}_0}
\]

The linearized system $\mathbf{u}' = J\mathbf{u}$ (where $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_0$) approximates behavior near $\mathbf{x}_0$.

If all eigenvalues of $J$ have nonzero real part, the nonlinear system has the same qualitative behavior as its linearization near $\mathbf{x}_0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Linearization of Predator-Prey
content.problem:
The predator-prey system
$$
x' &= x(1 - y) \\
y' &= y(x - 1)
$$
has equilibrium at $(1, 1)$.

Jacobian: $J = \begin{pmatrix} 1-y & -x \\ y & x-1 \end{pmatrix}$

At $(1,1)$: $J = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$

Eigenvalues: $\pm i$ (center)

The linearization predicts periodic orbits, and indeed the nonlinear system has closed trajectories around $(1,1)$.
